figure.head: Figure 8 . 14 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 8.14: (a) False-color scanning electron micrograph of device 2. The device consists of a superconducting Al NW on top of a InAs quantum well. The gates labeled V Pj can be used to tune the tunnel barrier between the tunnel probe j and the wire (j ∈ {1, 2, 3}). The gates labled V Bkl form the confining potential of the quasi one-dimensional NW at the bottom edge while also forming tunnel barriers. The gates labeled T kl confine the NW at the top edge and tune the chemical potential of individual NW segments (kl ∈ {01, 12, 23}). (c, d) Tunneling conductance measurements of G 1 , G 2 with V (j) SD = 0 V. States that couple strongly to the gate voltage V T12 are visible in both measurements. Two sharp resonances that strongly depend on the gate voltage V P2 appear in the measurement of G 2 . (b) Tunneling spectroscopy at V T12 = -4.67 V. The dashed lines serve as a guide to the eye. Tunneling spectroscopy at B || = 1.1 T, when moving the gate voltages along the green dashed lines at V P1 = 0.22 V and at V P2 = 0.05 V in (c, d) are plotted in (e, f). While the absolute values of tunneling conductance in G 1 and G 2 are different, the structure of the states in energy is nearly identical. A line cut at V (1) SD = V (2) SD = 0 V of the data in (e, f) is shown in (g).

figure.head: Figure 9 . 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.1: False-color electron micrograph of device 1. A proximitized quasi onedimensional NW is formed in the InAs quantum well (gray) under the strip of superconducting Al (blue) by lateral electrostatic confinement from the gates W L , W M , W R (red). Probes made from Al are separated by a tunnel barrier from the NW and allow for measurements of the tunneling currents I L , I R into the NW. The gates T L and T R (orange) tune the tunnel barriers between the NW and the probes.

three-terminal device and nonlocal conductance Device 1 is shown in Fig. 9.1. It consists of a superconducting strip of Al on top of a shallow 2DEG formed in an InAs quantum well. Two superconducting probes made from Al were defined 50 nm away from the superconducting strip. Gates made from Ti/Au are separated by HfO x gate dielectric from the semiconductor and superconductor. Details on the fabrication of device 1 are given in Appendix A.1. The gates labeled W L , W M , and W R cover different segments of the Al strip and electrostatically confine a quasi one-dimensional NW in the semiconductor. The gate T L (T R ) depletes the semiconductor between left (right) probe and NW forming a tunnel barrier. The ends of the superconducting strip were connected to electrically grounded planes of superconducting Al.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.2: Measurement of the four conductances as a function of magnetic field parallel to the NW B || at V WM = -3.02 V. Both local conductances in (a) and (d) reveal subgap states that cross zero bias. The subgap states also appear in the nonlocal conductances in (b) and (c).

evolution of andreev bound states and their charge character At a magnetic field of B || = 1.2 T, the ABSs trace out a pair of lobes which do not reach zero bias as a function of gate voltage V WM , as seen in the the local conductance G LL [see Fig. 9.3(a)]. The corresponding nonlocal conductance G LR , plotted in Fig. 9.3(b)

figure.head: Figure 9 . 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.3: Local conductance G LL and nonlocal conductance G LR as a function of V WM at three different values of magnetic field B || . (a) Subgap states appear as lobes in the superconducting gap. (b) The lowest excited state appears together with a spectrum of higher excited states in the nonlocal conductance. (c) At B || = 1.6 T the ABSs merge at zero bias. (d) The nonlocal conductance is suppressed around that region. (e) At B || = 2 T the ABSs intersect forming a low energy state that oscillates around zero bias. (f) The nonlocal conductance changes sign at the turning points of the low energy state.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.4: (a, b) Anti-symmetric and symmetric component of the nonlocal conductance G LR measured at B || = 2 T. (c, d) Anti-symmetric and symmetric part of the nonlocal conductances G LR , G RL extracted at the position of the lowest lying state marked by the green lines in (a, b). (e) Resulting values for Q L and Q R are approximately equal and show clear oscillations between +1 and -1. Positive (negative) values of Q j coincide with regions of positive (negative) slope of the state energy as a function of V WM .

state at energy E = eV j SD can be extracted from the anti-symmetric and symmetric components of the measured nonlocal conductance G ij (V j SD ) [119]. The symmetric and anti-symmetric components of the nonlocal conductance G LR measured at a magnetic field value B || = 2 T are plotted as a function of sourcedrain voltage in Figs. 9.4(a, b). The values stemming from the low energy state were extracted along the positions given by the green lines in Figs. 9.4(c, d). The extracted values of the (anti-)symmetric part of G LR (G RL ) are shown in Figs. 9.4(c, d) with a solid green (dotted black) line. These values correspond to the conductance values which enter Eq. 9.3. The (anti-)symmetric components G sym(anti) LR and G sym(anti) RL are approximately equal. The resulting values for Q L and Q R according to Eq. 9.3 are shown in Fig. 9.4(e). Q L closely follows Q R .

figure.head: Figure 9 . 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.5: Map of the sum of all conductances G sum at B || = 2 T. The inferred energies E inf L, R from the integrated Q L , Q R are superimposed. The E inf L, R curves roughly retrace the energy of the subgap state.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 6 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.6: Schematic of the electrical measurement setup. The sample was cooled in a dilution refrigerator. Connections to measurement electronics were made by electrical lines of impedance Z F . Two current to voltage amplifiers were used to measure the tunneling current across the tunnel barriers under the gates T L and T R . Four lock-in amplifiers in the shown configuration were used to measure the four elements of the conductance matrix. The lead under the gate T S was terminated by an open circuit at the breakout box.

figure.head: For
figure.figDesc: the extraction of the quantities Q j of a state at energy E, it is necessary to extract the values of G sym ij and G anti ij at the voltage eV j SD = E. Typically there is a local extremum in at least one of the measured conductance matrix elements around this voltage value. The routine used to determine this position was to search for local maxima in |G anti ij (, or |G sum (V SD )| for every value of gate voltage.

we show all four conductances as a function of gate voltage V WM at different magnetic field values B || . The data for G LL and G LR are shown in Section 9.3. ABSs appear as subgap states in both nonlocal conductances, and are visible in both local conductances G LL and G RR .

figure.head: Figure 9 . 7 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.7: Each row is a measurements of all four elements of the conductance matrix as a function of gate voltage V WM at a specific value of B || . The two leftmost columns are the data presented in the main text.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 8
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.8 shows the anti-symmetric parts of the local and nonlocal conductances for comparison. The underlying data is the same as in Fig. 9.7(a-d) taken at B || = 1.2 T. The data in Fig. 9.7(a, b) show -G anti LL and G anti LR . The two quantities are expected to be the same. The lowest excited state shows up with the same sign and similar strength in both plots. For energy values above the first excited state, the anti-symmetric part of the local conductance is larger than the anti-symmetric part of the nonlocal conductance. The data points from (a) and (b) are plotted parametrically in (c). The points that correspond to three pixels around the lowest excited state shown by the dashed line in (b) are shown as black dots in (c). They lie close to the green dashed line which corresponds to the relation predicted by theory. All other data points are plotted in grey. A large number of these points have a relatively small value in |G anti LR | compared to their relatively large value in |G anti LL |. These data points originate not only from energies above ∆, where a deviation from the relation in equation 9.5 is expected, but also from energies below ∆ and above ∆ ind .

figure.head: Figure 9 .
figure.figDesc: 8(f) confirms the observation of the lowest energy state fulfilling the expected relation from Eq. 9.5.

(d, e) comparison of -G anti RR and G anti RL . (f) shows a parametric plot of data points from (d, e) with the data points taken from a three pixel window around the dashed line in (e) shown in black.

figure.figDesc: Figure 9.10: (a) tunneling conductance G LL , (b) the sum of all conductance matrix elements G sum , (c) tunneling conductance G RR measured at B || = 1.2 T. Subgap states around V WM = -3.03 V and V WM = -3.025 V appear symmetric with respect to V SD in G sum , but show a notable asymmetry in G LL and G RR . Extracted value of Q L and Q R For the nonlocal conductances measured at 1.2 T shown in Fig. 9.7(b, d) we extracted

figure.head: . 8 )Figure 9 . 11 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.11: (a-d) symmetric and anti-symmetric components of the nonlocal conductances measured at B || = 1.2 T. The values at the position of the green and black line are plotted in (e, f) and were used to extract Q L , Q R , which are shown in (g). The two evolve similarly with V WM . An abrupt change from positive to negative values is seen where neighbouring ABS cross. (h) shows the sum of all conductances G sum . The dotted black, solid green lines display the energies E inf L, R inferred from the integrated Q L , Q R . They roughly retrace the bound state energy.

figure.head: Figure 9 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.12 shows a measurement of local and nonlocal conductance as a function of magnetic field B ⊥ oriented out of the 2DEG plane. The direction of the magnetic field is denoted in Fig. 9.6. The critical field value of the Al film for this magnetic field direction is lowered in comparison to the in plane magnetic field B || . The superconducting gap therefore closes at a magnetic field value around 0.22 T before subgap states enter the superconducting energy gap. The nonlocal conductance is restricted to a small region close to the gap of the superconductor. A supercurrent is visible at V SD = 0 V for field values |B ⊥ | < 0.1 T. This can be explained by the wire and the leads being both in the superconducting state. The supercurrent appears clearly in the local conductances G LL and G RR , but does not appear in the nonlocal conductances G LR and G RL . This is a result of the supercurrent being mediated by Cooper pairs that go from the superconducting leads directly to the superconducting condensate of the nanowire. Nonlocal conductance, on the other hand, is mediated by 1e quasiparticles that are transmitted and Andreev reflected from one lead to the other.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 12 : 9 . 5 . 6
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.12: Measurement of all four conductances as a function of magnetic field B ⊥ pointing out of the 2DEG plane. (a, c) The local conductances show a clear supercurrent as a peak at zero source drain bias. The supercurrent does not appear in the nonlocal conductances in (b, d).

with the gate voltage V WR = -3.09 V -significantly more positive than the neighbouring gates. The ABS appears as subgap states in both local conductances G LL and G RR . The states emerge from the continuum of QP at an energy ∆ at low magnetic field B || ≈ 0.3 T and cross zero bias at B || ≈ 1.6 T. The nonlocal conductances G LR and G RL are appreciably large at the energies of the low energy ABS and above. The signal is furthermore strongly suppressed for source drain voltages above the parent gap eV SD > ∆. The nonlocal conductance shows a strong anti-symmetric component and the subgap states originating from the low energy ABS show a characteristic pattern as they cross zero energy. Specifically, the states cross without a change in sign of the nonlocal conductance. Similar behavior has been observed previously in numerical studies and experiments [41, 119, 134].

figure.head: Figure 9 . 13 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.13: Measurement of all four conductances as a function of magnetic field B || . (a, c) Local conductances reveal an ABS in the form of subgap states that cross zero source drain bias. (b, d) ABSs and higher excited states appear with sizeable nonlocla conductance.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 14 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.14: Each row shows all four conductances as a function of gate voltage V WR at a specific value of B || . (a-d) ABSs in the NW appear as lobe shaped subgap states that appear in both local conductances and nonlocal conductances at low magnetic fields. (e-h) With increasing filed B || the ABS are lowered in energy.(i-l) The subgap states merged and cross at zero bias.

figure.head: Figure 9 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.15(a-d) shows the symmetric and anti-symmetric components of G LR and G RL measured at B || = 1.2 T. The values at the position of the lowest lying state

figure.head: Figure 9 . 15 :

The values at the position of the green and black line are plotted in (e, f) and were used to calculate Q L , Q R , which are plotted in (g). The two quantities evolve similarly with V WM . (h) shows the sum of all conductances G sum . The dotted black, solid green lines display the energies E inf L, R inferred from the integrated Q L , Q R .

figure.head: Figure 9 . 16 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.16: False-color micrograph of device 2. A wire connected to electrical ground planes at its ends is formed by the Al (blue) on top of the InAs quantum well (gray). Electrostatic gates (red) control the electron density in the NW and control the quantum point contacts adjacent to the NW.

(b, d)], the closing of the induced gap is visible at B || ≈ 1 T where the low energy ABSs merge at zero bias. Higher excited subgap states are resolved in the nonlocal conductance. At voltages above the parent gap |eV SD | > ∆ finite nonlocal conductance is visible.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 17 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.17: Local and nonlocal conductances as a function of magnetic field measured on device 2. The parent gap ∆ is visible at high source drain bias in (a, c). The strongly reduced induced gap ∆ ind is visible in all four conductance matrix elements and closes around B || ≈ 1 T.

9.18(a, c). At the voltage eV SD = ∆ ind ≈ 80 µeV the states go through a minimum in energy. Similar excited state spectra have been observed in numerical simulations[123, 133]. The presence of disorder, localized bound states in the NW, and multiple transverse modes may further affect the excited state spectrum. The lobe shaped ABSs lead to nonlocal conductance signal as seen from Fig. 9.18(b, d) with changes in sign around their minimal energy ≈ 80 µeV. With increasing magnetic field, the ABSs lower their energy. At a magnetic field value B || = 0.65 T some of the ABSs merge at zero bias [Fig. 9.18 (e, g)]. At B || = 1.1 T the ABSs have crossed zero bias [see Fig. 9.18(i, k)]. Around V WM = -4.8 V, subgap states oscillate around zero bias in Fig. 9.18(i, k). Most subgap states in Fig. 9.18 appear both in the local and nonlocal conductances, which is a strong indication that they stem from extended bound states that couple to the normal leads at both ends of the NW segment.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 18 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.18: Measured conductance matrix elements at different magnetic field values. (a-d) the induced gap is visible in local and nonlocal conductances. The excited state spectrum consists of discrete states due to the finite size of the NW segment. (i-l) states have crossed zero bias and a low energy state that oscillates in energy is visible.

figure.head: Figure 9 . 19 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9.19: Local and nonlocal conductances at different values of B || . The subgap state close to zero bias in (c) is not visible in the three other conductances measured at the same field value. Some of the lobe shaped ABSs appear in both local conductances (a, c) and in the nonlocal conductances (b, d) with the characteristic sign changes. (e-h) subgap state around V WM = -4.67 V oscillates around zero bias and is visible in all local and nonlocal conductances.

A particularly large anti-symmetric component of the local conductance G RR is visible at low values of source-drain bias |V R SD | ≤ 80 µV due to the presence of the low energy subgap state that only appears in G RR as discussed earlier. A parametric plot of the data in (d, e) is shown in (f) and confirms the lack of points close to the expected theory relation given by the green dashed line.

The gates W kl , kl ∈ {01, 13, 34} are used to deplete the 2DEG self-aligned with a strip of epitaxial Al and tune the density in individual segments of the NW. Superconducting

figure.head: Figure 10 . 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.3: Differential conductance traces for a NW with localized states at its ends and a NW with extended states. figure.head: Figure 10 . 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.4: Bias spectroscopy at the ends and in the middle of the NW as a function of magnetic field parallel to the NW. The gate voltages were set to the values given by the blue in Fig.10.2. (a) Conductance at the left end of the NW segment shows a persisting zero-bias peak at high magnetic fields. (b) spectroscopy in the open transport regime shows no discrete states, but a finite gap at high magnetic fields is visible. (c) Spectroscopy at the right NW end shows states crossing and merging at zero bias with increasing field. (d) Peak locations of the low energy subgap states extracted from (a) and (c). Linear fit yielding g factor of 4.2 shown as dashed line. (e) Parametric plot of the peak locations from (d). Identity line shown in solid black.

They are pinned around zero bias for an extended region. (b) The probe in the middle of the NW shows subgap states overshooting around B || = 2 T. figure.head: Figure 10 . 7 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.7: Zero-bias conductance as a function of the gate voltage that tunes the electron density in the NW and the gate voltage that controls the tunnel barrier. (a, b) A resonance that depends weakly on V P1 and V P2 is visible in tunneling conductance at both ends of the NW. The states show a similar slope and shape. (c, d) The same measurement repeated for a different value of the voltage on gate B01 which is adjacent to the quantum point contact on the left. The state in the tunneling conductance at the left NW end in (c) is located at more positive gate voltage V T12 now in comparison to (a), while the state measured on the right end in (d) is only shifted slightly in comparison to (b). Tunneling spectroscopy along the dashed lines is shown in supplementary figure 10.16.

figure.head: Fig. 10 .
figure.figDesc: Fig.10.8(a, b), the ABSs appear as subgap states in both G 1 and G 2 . While the states show a similar behavior as a function of V T12 , they do not coincide as the line cut in Fig.10.8(c) illustrates. For the case V B01 = -0.81 V shown in Fig.10.8(d, e) the ABSs are moved with respect to each other and the regions where they are close to zero bias overlap [see Fig.10.8(f)]. This illustrates how end-to-end correlations as a function of one parameter can be produced by fine-tuning a second parameter.

figure.head: Figure 10 . 8 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.8: Tunneling spectroscopy as a function of gate voltage V T12 which tunes the electron density in the NW segment. (a) Tunneling conductance on the left NW end shows ABSs emerging from the continuum and forming a zero-bias conductance peak. (b) Tunneling conductance on the rigth NW end shows very similar behavior. The states do not appear at identical values of V T12 which is also seen in the zero-bias cuts shown in (c). (d) For a more negative gate voltage V B01 = -0.81 V, the subgap states measured on the left NW end appears at more positive values of V T12 . (e) The evolution of the state measured on the right is unaffected by the change in V B01 . There is now a finite range of V T12 where the states appear correlated as seen in the zero-bias line cuts in (f).

figure.head: Figure 10 . 9 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.9: Bias spectroscopy of two distinct ABSs at fine-tuned gate voltage value as a function of magnetic field parallel to the NW. (a) Tunneling conductance on the left shows states merging and forming a zero-bias peak at high magnetic field. (b) Tunneling conductance on the right NW end shows subgap states with identical evolution to the one in (a). (c) The extracted peak positions from (a) and (b) lie on top of each other. The dashed line shows a linear fit with g factor 3.1. (d) Parametric plot of extracted peaks from (c) with the identity line shown in solid black.

figure.head: Figure 10 . 10 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.10: Correlation analysis of localized low energy states at the two NW ends in comparison to state extending throughout the NW. (a, b) Parametric plot of peak positions of states localized at the ends of a long (1.5 µm) NW. The peak positions are from Fig. 10.4 and Fig. 10.5 respectively. (c) Parametric plot of fine-tuned, localized states from Fig. 10.9. (d) Parametric plot from Fig. 8.5 of the peak positions for extended states in a short (0.6 µm) NW. The points lie close to the identity line (solid black) which indicates perfect correlations. The difference between correlations due to fine-tuned localized states and due to extended bound states may be small and subtle to recognize.

figure.head: Figure 10 . 11 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10.11: Zero-bias conductance as a function of the gate T 12 that tunes the electron density in the NW and magnetic field parallel to the NW. (a,b) An ellipse shape of conductance can be seen in conductance measured on both the left and the right NW end as a result of the evolution with V T12 in Fig. 10.15. The ellipses appear roughly at the gate voltage due to fine-tuning of V B01 and V H1 . (c,d) the third harmonic of the tunneling current as a measure for peaks around zero bias confirm the ellipse shaped region hosts zero-bias conductance peaks.

figure.head: Figure 11 . 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.1: False-color scanning electron micrograph of an example three-terminal device based on full-shell nanowires. (a) InAs nanowire (white) after selective etch of the Al shell (blue) and deposition of ohmic contacts (orange). (b) completed device with superconducting contact (purple) and topgates T L and T R (yellow).

figure.head: Figure 11 .
figure.figDesc: 1(a) shows a full-shell Al-InAs NW after removing the Al at the NW ends by wet etching. Both ends are contacted by metal contacts, leaving a short segment of semiconducting NW between the metal contact and the Al covered part of the NW. A superconducting contact can be used to electrically ground the superconducting shell. A technique that uses a ramp fabricated from cross-linked resist next to the NW, removal of the native oxide of the Al shell by ion milling, and deposition of a thin layer of Al was developed in Ref.[154] and was adapted for the present work. A completed device can be seen in 11.1(b). The NW ends are covered by gate dielectric and top gates T L and T R . A back gate is provided by the doped silicon/silicon substrate. The top gates combined with the back gate allow for gating of the InAs NW from all sides to form a tunnel barrier.In the following, measurements of local and nonlocal tunneling conductance for two devices are presented. The length of the Al covered part of the NW was L ≈ 2.1 µm for device 5 and L ≈ 2.6 µm for device 6. Both NW had a semiconducting core diameter of ≈ 80 nm and ≈ 5 nm thick Al shell. The lock-in detection technique as described in Ref.[119] was used. The experimental setup is sketched in Fig.11.1(b).

figure.head: Figure 11 . 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.2: Gate dependence of conductance resonances at zero source-drain voltage in the first lobe. (a, b) Conductance resonances as a function of both backgate and respective topgate voltage at both nanowire ends. (c, d) Zoom-in on the data inside the green rectangles in (a, b). Markers denote the gate configuration of measurements shown below.

figure.head: Figure 11 . 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.3: Bias spectroscopy as function of parallel magnetic field. (a-d) All four conductance matrix elements with the gate voltages at the values denoted by in Fig. 11.2. While a gap is visible in the zeroth lobe in all four measurements, no gap is seen in the first lobe. (e-h) the full conductance matrix at gate voltages denoted by in Fig. 11.2. G RR in (g) shows conductance peaks ≈ 0.02 meV below the superconducting gap at zero magnetic field and a strong ZBP in the first lobe. (i-l) All four conductance matrix elements with gate voltages at the values given by in Fig. 11.2. G RR in (k) shows two strong conductance resonances close to zero bias in the zeroth lobe that evolve into a ZBP in the first lobe.

figure.head: Figure 11 . 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.4: Bias spectroscopy as a function of magnetic field and comparison of the spectra measured in G LL and G RR . (a-d) Measurement of all four conductance matrix elements at gate voltage values denoted by G in Fig. 11.2. (e-g) comparison of line cuts of G LL and G RR at the three magnetic field values. The spectra are similar in the number and density of conductance peaks. The position of the peaks in G LL and G RR do not occur at the exact same value of V SD , in particular the low-energy states in the first lobe shown in (f,g).

No obvious QD states are visible in the local conductances G LL and G RR . The number and density of subgap states is similar, however. This is confirmed by the line cuts at different values of magnetic field [see Figs. 11.4(e-g)].

figure.head: Figure 11 . 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.5: Bias spectroscopy as function of magnetic field and topgate voltages. (a-d) Measurement of the all four conductance matrix elements as a function of magnetic field at gate voltages denoted by N in Fig. 11.2. (e, f) Local tunneling conductances as a function of left topgate voltage around the gate voltage values denoted by N in Fig. 11.2. There is no change in the local conductance measured on the right, except for electrostatic switches. (g,h) Measurement of local tunneling conductances as a function of right topgate voltage. The spectrum of subgap states in G RR changes due to energy level hybridization with quantum dot resonances. The spectrum in G LL stays constant.

figure.head: Figure 11 . 6 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11.6: Bias spectroscopy as a function of magnetic field measured on a different device. (a-d) Full conductance matrix at zero backgate voltage. An enhancement of zero bias conductance is visible in both local conductances, no gap is visible in the nonlocal conductances. (e-h) All four conductance matrix elements at more negative backgate voltage. The spectra on the two local conductances show close similarity, with a difference in the lowest excited states.

figure.head: Figure 12 . 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 12.1: (a) False-color micrograph of NW device with an array of finger-gate electrodes. An Al strip (blue) defines the NW and can be gate controlled by wide gates (red) on its upper edge and by an array of finger gates (orange and red) on its lower edge. Putting a single finger-gate to a voltage around zero volt allows for tunneling between the NW and the elongated leads (blue) next to the NW. Part V A P P E N D I X A FA B R I C AT I O N P R O T O C O L S The following gives detailed fabrication protocols of the devices presented in this work. a.1 fabrication of sideprobe device with superconducting probes Device 1 and device 4 were fabricated according to this protocol. • Cleaving -Wafer is coated with post-baked A4 PMMA resist.
Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate 5 nm Ti . * Evaporate 20 nm Au. * Evaporate 5 nm Ti . -Liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane overinight, rinse with aceton squirt, rinse with IPA squirt, blowdry.

Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate 10 nm Ti at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 30 nm Au at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 300 nm Au at 0°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 50 nm Au at 10°tilt and medium rotation.-Liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane overinight, rinse with aceton squirt, rinse with IPA squirt, blowdry.• Atomic layer deposition of second gate dielectric -Pump down for 10 h at 90 °C.

Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate 10 nm Ti at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 30 nm Au at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 300 nm Au at 0°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 50 nm Au at 10°tilt and medium rotation. -Liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane overinight, rinse with aceton squirt, rinse with IPA squirt, blowdry. a.3 fabrication of sideprobe device with array of gate electrodes
Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate 3 nm Ti . * Evaporate 27 nm Al. -Liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane overinight, rinse with aceton squirt, rinse with IPA squirt, blowdry.

5 nA, dose time = 0.32 µs/dot [proximity effect correction for InP substrate, 200 nm PMMA, 100 kV, optimal contrast]. -EBL of coarse features with 600 µm write field, 20000 dots, pitch 1. Beam current 20 nA, dose time = 0.3 µs/dot [proximity effect correction for InP substrate, 200 nm PMMA, 100 kV, optimal contrast]. -Develop 45 s MIPA:IPA (1:3), 5 s IPA, blowdry. Ash for 60 s. Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate 10 nm Ti at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 30 nm Au at 5°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 300 nm Au at 0°tilt and medium rotation. * Evaporate 50 nm Au at 10°tilt and medium rotation. -Liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane overinight, rinse with aceton squirt, rinse with IPA squirt, blowdry.a.4 fabrication of fullshell nanowire three-terminal device Device 5 and device 6 were fabricated according to this protocol.• Nanowire deposition -Clean carrier chip for 5 min in aceton, 5 min in IPA, blowdry.

Metal deposition in AJA * Kaufmann milling at medium rotation, 15 sccm Ar, 1 mTorr, 100 V, 5 min discharge, 2 min warm up, 8 min milling. * Let sample cool down for 5 min. * Evaporate at medium rotation 5 nm Ti, 200 nm Au. -Liftoff for 5 min at 65 °C in NMP, 1 min at room temperature in aceton, 1 min at room temperature in IPA, blowdry.

Metal deposition in AJA * Kaufmann milling at medium rotation, 15 sccm Ar, 1 mTorr, 100 V, 5 min discharge, 2 min warm up, 4.5 min milling. * Let sample cool down for 5 min. * Evaporate 25 nm Al. -Liftoff for 5 min at 80 °C in NMP, 1 min at room temperature in aceton, 1 min at room temperature in IPA, blowdry. • Atomic layer deposition of gate dielectric -Pump down for 10 h at 90 °C. -70 cycles of TDMAH precursor pulse, 150 s waiting time, and H 2 O pulse at 90 °C.

9.3 Evolution of Andreev bound states and their charge character

figure.figDesc: 3.2(c)]. This is analogous to the Kitaev chain with the only difference of a periodic modulation, which arises due to the spin and particle-hole structure of the states in the presence of spin-orbit coupling and magnetic field [115, 150, 176].3.1.3 Signatures of Majorana zero modesInspired by the Lutchyn-Oreg model, multiple experiments attempted to realize a topological phase hosting MZMs in semiconducting NWs proximitized by a superconductor. Semiconducting InAs in combination with superconducting Al has been among the used material combinations. In such systems, multiple signatures of MZMs have been predicted by theory, most of which have been observed in experiments. Based on the current status quo of this research field, none of these experiments could convincingly distinguish between MZMs and trivial ABSs, as both can give rise to the same experimental signatures.

The four lines that are used to connect the ground planes of the sample to the breakout box are represented by a single blue line. The dilution refrigerator is an Oxford Instruments Triton 400.

Figure 8.7: The three current-to-voltage (I/V) converting amplifiers are made by Basel Pre-
cision Instruments (part number SP983c). Source-drain bias voltage is supplied
to the sample via the input voltage offset port of the I/V converters. Suitable
resistors together with the input resistance of this port form a voltage divider.
The lock-in amplifiers are made by Stanford Research Systems (model SR830

figure.figDesc: material system that potentially hosts Majorana zero modes (MZMs) are vapor-liquid-solid grown InAs nanowires with a shell of superconducting Al that covers the full nanowire (NW) surface. In this chapter, the realization of a three terminal device based on full-shell Al-InAs NWs is presented. The device geometry allows for tunneling spectroscopy at both ends of a NW and measurements of nonlocal conductance. A rich structure of subgap states is observed. The spectra of conductance resonances on both NW ends bear similarity but are not identical. When hybridizing subgap states with local quantum dot states in the tunnel barrier on one NW end, the spectrum is unaffected on the other NW end. This is in disagreement with states that are extending through the whole NW. No signatures of a gap reopening that would be expected for a topological phase have been observed in nonlocal conductance measurements. Quantum dot states can furthermore dominate the local conductance spectra and lead to strong zero-bias conductance enhancements mimicking the signatures of a topological phase.The NWs for the study presented in the following were provided by the Krogstrup group at the University of Copenhagen. Device Fabrication was done by M. E. Kloster, and A. Pöschl. E. B. Hansen, and A. Pöschl contributed equally to measurements and data analysis.

Metal deposition in AJA * Evaporate at medium rotation 5 nm Ti, 100 nm Au with no tilt. Evaporate at medium rotation 80 nm Au with no tilt.-Liftoff for 7 min at 80 °C in NMP, 1 min at room temperature in aceton, 1 min at room temperature in IPA, blowdry.

note.p: * Evaporate at medium rotation 30 nm Au at 10°tilt. *

div.p: af BAT. tilstande. tunnelkonduktans, målt ved hver ende af NL når den omkringliggende superledende Al cylinder er trådet af en kvanteflux. Tilstande som udstraekker sig langs hele NL blev eksperimentelt udelukket. Dog fører undergabs tilstandene til ens spektre i begge ender, hvilket antyder at NL's tvaersnitsform staerkt influerer det endelig spektrum.
-Clean and liftoff in 1,3-dioxolane for 5 min under ultrasonication at 80 kHz. Rinse with squirt of aceton, rinse with squirt of IPA, blowdry.
• Mesa etch -Spin coat with A4 PMMA for 45 s at 4000 rpm, post-bake 120 s at 185 °C.

-EBL of fine features with 150 µm write field, 60000 dots, pitch 1. Beam current 0.5 nA, dose time = 0.085 µs/dot [proximity effect correction for InAs substrate, 200 nm PMMA, 100 kV, optimal contrast].

-EBL of coarse features with 600 µm write field, 20000 dots, pitch 1. Beam current 0.5 nA, dose time = 0.34 µs/dot [proximity effect correction for InAs substrate, 200 nm PMMA, 100 kV, optimal contrast].
-Develop 60 s in MIPA:IPA (1:3), 5 s IPA, blowdry.
-Ash for 60 s.
